attributes.stem:
A 53-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic for review six weeks after coronary artery bypass graft surgery. He has a medical history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypercholesterolaemia, and previous inferior myocardial infarction. His medications include aspirin, metoprolol, perindopril, atorvastatin, metformin, and insulin glargine. He holds a private driver licence and was advised he could resume driving after at least four weeks following surgery if there were no complications.

On examination, he appears well. His pulse rate is 65/min, blood pressure 125/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 14/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.5°C. His sternotomy wound is well-healed with no signs of infection. There are no signs of heart failure. His cardiac rehabilitation is progressing well.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is most likely to preclude him from resuming driving?
options: Occasional mild sternal discomfort on movement
attributes.explanation:
This patient has had an uncomplicated recovery from coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) surgery and is now six weeks post-procedure. The ability to safely operate a motor vehicle depends on multiple factors including cardiac symptoms, wound healing, and overall functional status.

Episodes of angina at rest would be a significant contraindication to resuming driving. Ongoing cardiac symptoms suggest incomplete revascularisation or new coronary issues that could impair a person's ability to safely control a vehicle. Such symptoms require urgent medical review and likely cardiac investigation.

Non-attendance at cardiac rehabilitation, while not ideal for optimal recovery, is not an absolute contraindication to driving. The focus is on the patient's functional capacity and symptom status rather than rehabilitation attendance.

Requirement for ongoing insulin therapy does not automatically disqualify someone from driving a private vehicle, provided their diabetes is well-controlled and they understand the risks and management of hypoglycaemia.

Blood pressure of 165/85 mmHg is mildly elevated but would not prevent someone from driving. Significant hypertension that could cause end-organ damage or acute symptoms would be of greater concern.

Occasional mild sternal discomfort on movement is common after CABG surgery and would not preclude driving if the person can perform emergency manoeuvres and wear a seatbelt comfortably.

The general principles for returning to driving after CABG include: adequate wound healing, no significant cardiac symptoms, ability to perform emergency manoeuvres, and clearance from the treating physician. Commercial drivers typically require a longer period (usually three months) before returning to driving due to the greater demands of their occupation. Austroads guidelines also stipulates that post CABG, patients cannot hold unconditional licences and will need periodical medical review to hold a conditional licence.
learningPoints: After uncomplicated CABG surgery, patients must wait at least 4 weeks before resuming driving.
Ongoing and frequent episodes of chest pain, palpitations, breathlessness or poor wound healing are contraindications to resuming driving after CABG.
Commercial drivers require longer periods (typically 3 months) before returning to driving after CABG.
references: Austroads and National Transport Commission. Assessing Fitness to Drive for Commercial and Private Vehicle Drivers – Medical Standards for Licensing and Clinical Management Guidelines. 2022. Available from URL: https://austroads.com.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0037/498691/AP-G56-22_Assessing_Fitness_Drive.pdf

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree

attributes.name: Rehabilitation following coronary artery bypass graft
